answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: Scenario 1 Orderly decarbonisation | Scenario 2 Regional rivalry | Scenario 3 Hothouse | SSP/RCP combination used: SSP1 RCP1.9 & 2.6 | SSP3 RCP4.5 | SSP5 RCP8.5 | Warming level: 1.5°C warming by 2100 | 2.9°C warming by 2100 | 4.8°C warming by 2100
answers.notes: Three scenarios explicitly named and tabulated with SSP/RCP combinations and warming levels.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes by 2100 are described: 1.5°C (Orderly decarbonisation, SSP1/RCP1.9 & 2.6), 2.9°C (Regional rivalry, SSP3/RCP4.5), and 4.8°C (Hothouse, SSP5/RCP8.5). No temperature overshoot is mentioned.
answers.evidence: Warming level: 1.5°C warming by 2100 | 2.9°C warming by 2100 | 4.8°C warming by 2100 ... Rise in average global temperatures in the 2081-2100 period relative to the preindustrial baseline (1850-1900)
answers.notes: Temperatures are explicitly stated as rises in average global temperatures in the 2081-2100 period relative to the preindustrial baseline (1850-1900). No overshoot language used.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple providers are named: The Aotearoa Circle (Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios), NZCS (New Zealand Climate Standards), and Aurecon Limited (advisory support). The underlying SSP/RCP frameworks are attributed to the IPCC.
answers.evidence: In selecting its three scenarios, T&G has been guided by the requirements of the NZCS and The Aotearoa Circle's Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios. ... In 2023, T&G, supported by Aurecon Limited, an Asia Pacific design, engineering and advisory company, conducted robust qualitative analysis to develop its climate scenarios... RCP: Adopted by the IPCC... SSP: Adopted by the IPCC
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.8; Appendix 4 Glossary, p.21
answers.notes: The Aotearoa Circle provided the sector-level scenario framework; Aurecon provided advisory support; NZCS mandated the scenario requirements; IPCC underpins the SSP/RCP frameworks.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Scenario 1 Orderly decarbonisation: SSP1, RCP1.9 & 2.6 | Scenario 2 Regional rivalry: SSP3, RCP4.5 | Scenario 3 Hothouse: SSP5, RCP8.5 ... Our second scenario, regional rivalry (2.9°C), differs from the sector scenarios. It was selected as we consider regional rivalry (SSP3, RCP4.5) to be the more comprehensive challenge for our business... We selected RCP4.5 because: Of the availability of RCP4.5 data relative to RCP6.0 data (currently, there is very little data for RCP6.0 downscaled to regions in Aotearoa New Zealand).
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, Table 2 and Rationale for these scenarios, p.8
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP and RCP identifiers for each scenario. Scenario 2 (SSP3 + RCP4.5) is a cross-family combination — SSP3 is typically paired with RCP6.0 in the standard IPCC AR6 framework, but T&G explicitly chose RCP4.5 instead. The document acknowledges this deviation and provides a rationale.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: Using The Aotearoa Circle's Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios as a basis, a series of workshops were held with the working group to: Agree upon T&G's value chain and key value drivers. Define and develop T&G's three scenarios and supporting narratives for plausible socio-economic, technological, environmental and political futures. Discuss and identify the potential physical and transitional risks and opportunities, under each of the three scenarios. ... This was done by exploring: What physical and transitional risks and opportunities would be plausible for T&G's business model? When would they materialise, where in the value chain might they occur, and how specifically in T&G's industry context might they take place?
answers.notes: The scenarios were adapted from The Aotearoa Circle's Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios and tailored to T&G's specific value chain and industry context through workshops. The document also notes that Scenario 2 (Regional rivalry) was specifically modified from the sector scenarios to better reflect T&G's business challenges. The mention of "downscaled to regions in Aotearoa New Zealand" also indicates localisation of climate data.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: Over 30 T&G team members from across our global value chain, together with a representative from The New Zealand Institute for Plant & Food Research, were brought together to form a working group. ... In developing its sector scenarios, The Aotearoa Circle brought together the diversity of the agriculture sector to collaborate, share knowledge, science and insights, and inform the outcome. T&G participated as a member of the Technical Expert Group.
answers.notes: There is clear evidence of participatory/collaborative scenario development: (1) T&G participated in The Aotearoa Circle's collaborative sector scenario development as a Technical Expert Group member; (2) T&G's own scenario development involved 30+ team members from across the value chain plus external experts in workshops.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.evidence: In 2023, T&G conducted its first climate scenario analysis to develop three plausible scenarios which describe how the future may develop, each supported by a set of assumptions. These scenarios are not predictions of the future, they are hypothetical outcomes which challenge our understanding and help us build further resilience into our strategy and business model.
answers.notes: No explicit baseline or reference scenario is described against which the three climate scenarios are compared. The document describes three forward-looking scenarios but does not identify any of them as a "baseline" or "business as usual" reference scenario for comparison purposes. The Hothouse scenario could be interpreted as a high-emissions baseline, but it is not explicitly labelled as such.

answers.question: Model used
answers.notes: No specific climate models, integrated assessment models (IAMs), or macroeconomic models are named in the document. The analysis is described as qualitative, supported by Aurecon Limited. The SSP/RCP frameworks are referenced but no specific models (e.g., CLIMADA, REMIND, MESSAGE, GCAM, NiGEM) are named.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Damage to T&G operations due to increasing intensity and frequency of severe weather events ... Orchards are vulnerable to hail ... Timely harvesting of fruit and fruit quality is impacted by heavy rain ... Localised impact to Hawke's Bay due to Cyclone Gabrielle. Impact on growing operations and assets associated with these operations ... The severity and frequency of ex-tropical cyclones, flooding, drought and fire increases in Aotearoa New Zealand, which creates catastrophic growing damage, significant losses and supply chain disruptions.
answers.location_hint: Risk Management section, Table 4, p.14; Strategy section, p.10
answers.notes: The document explicitly includes damage and loss estimates from acute physical risks (extreme weather events such as cyclones, flooding, hail, drought, fire) as a material climate-related risk. Cyclone Gabrielle is cited as a specific example of a 1-in-70 year event with actual impact on operations.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: Physical risks emanating from climate change can be event-driven (acute) such as increased severity of extreme weather events. They can also relate to longer-term shifts (chronic) in precipitation and temperature and increased variability in weather patterns, such as sea level rise. ... The severity and frequency of ex-tropical cyclones, flooding, drought and fire increases in Aotearoa New Zealand ... Globally, water stress and competition, and energy and food instability cause poverty and political instability ... SSP: Adopted by the IPCC, Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSPs) are projections which describe alternative futures of socio-economic development in the absence of climate policy intervention. They include a wide range of drivers, including gross domestic product, population size, urbanisation and human and technological development.
answers.location_hint: Risk Management section p.13; Strategy section p.10; Appendix 4 Glossary p.21
answers.notes: Variables are drawn from multiple parts of the document — the scenario narratives, risk tables, and glossary definitions. Not all variables are explicitly listed as "scenario drivers" in a single place; some are described as risk impacts within the scenarios.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: Of the availability of RCP4.5 data relative to RCP6.0 data (currently, there is very little data for RCP6.0 downscaled to regions in Aotearoa New Zealand). ... T&G defines the time horizons to assess climate-related risks and opportunities in accordance with the United Nations' Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA)...
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.8; Risk Management section, p.11
answers.notes: The document references global SSP/RCP scenarios as the basis, but also explicitly mentions the use of data "downscaled to regions in Aotearoa New Zealand" and references NIWA (New Zealand's national climate research institute) for time horizon definitions, indicating both global and regional/local scales are used.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: 1–3 years; Medium-term: 3–10 years; Long-term: 10–50 years (with wider scenarios extending to 2100). Rationales are explicitly provided for each timeframe.
answers.evidence: Short-term 1–3 years (this aligns to business planning and capital allocation processes) | Medium-term 3–10 years (this aligns to internal business planning as well as global climate change ambitions under the Paris Agreement 2030) | Long-term 10–50 years (this aligns to apple orchard growing cycles of 25 years, the life span of major assets and mid/end century time horizons used by both IPCC and NIWA). ... This analysis looked at the next 50 years (2073) which aligns with the lifetime of T&G's assets and Aotearoa New Zealand's regulatory aspirations for net zero by 2050. The wider scenarios examined however extend to 2100.
answers.notes: All three timeframes are explicitly defined and justified. Short-term aligns to business planning; medium-term aligns to Paris Agreement 2030 goals; long-term aligns to apple orchard growing cycles (25 years), asset lifespan, and IPCC/NIWA mid/end-century horizons.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: In selecting its three scenarios, T&G has been guided by the requirements of the NZCS and The Aotearoa Circle's Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios. ... Our second scenario, regional rivalry (2.9°C), differs from the sector scenarios. It was selected as we consider regional rivalry (SSP3, RCP4.5) to be the more comprehensive challenge for our business, given the aspects of deglobalisation, increased national food security and the subsequent effects on consumer preferences and market size. We selected RCP4.5 because: Of the availability of RCP4.5 data relative to RCP6.0 data (currently, there is very little data for RCP6.0 downscaled to regions in Aotearoa New Zealand). It is commonly adopted by other reporting entities, allowing for easier comparison of Climate-related Disclosures. It was used in The Aotearoa Circle's sector scenarios, allowing consistency in the translation of sector-to-company specific scenarios. ... Chosen to reflect a plausible future in which T&G has to rapidly decarbonise and transition its operations [Scenario 1] | Selected as it poses high levels of disruption to international trade that would affect T&G's business model [Scenario 2] | Elected due to the high level of physical impact that would manifest in T&G's growing operations [Scenario 3]
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, Table 2 and Rationale for these scenarios, p.8
answers.notes: Explicit justifications are provided for each scenario choice and for the specific RCP selection (RCP4.5 over RCP6.0). The rationale covers regulatory compliance (NZCS), data availability, comparability with other entities, and business-specific relevance.
